replies.author: @IllisiaAdams
replies.text:
​@mikefalderoff8236 If they skipped season two, we wouldn't have Strange New Worlds!

That said, *I* know how to fix Discovery! Drumroll, please... Make the "main character" (can't recall her name) *not* the main character lol! It's Star Trek. It's *not supposed* to have a main character. It's always been a *team* of characters, right from TOS. Oh, and make her *no longer a 'Mary Sue'* (or, as my friend calls her, "Space Jesus" hahahaha!) who can do literally *almost anything* which makes her just a *bad character* tbh, and it leaves the rest of the crew/characters *nothing to blooming DO!* 🤦‍♀️ There. I fixed it. 

PS. Maybe also erase the Spock brother plot. A loooot of fans of the OG show and movies didn't like that retcon and thought it was a *betrayal* of Spock.

Voyager was my least favorite of the Berman shows and I'd snarf that puppy up in a second.  After DS9, of course.  The whole reason for this is that the Next Gen set didn't sell as much as they wanted it to.  That's what I read anyway.  And that one looks and sounds great.  Paramount/CBS has never really known what to do with the franchise and it shows, especially from the time Les Moonves killed Enterprise to now.

author: @999benhonda
text: I hope this is a sign that the skydance merger is resulting in the dimwits at the top being ousted.  They have allowed Kurtzman to keep reigns he had no business having.  Anyone with a brain should easily see that a cheesy, coming of age show with an expensive scifi budget is just dumb.  Trek, and its requisite budget, is meant for a much bigger story, a broader theme, made to reach a wider audience.   The idea that trek needs a show or shows for younger audiences just shows how far off the rails the franchise has gone...I grew up watching tng, ds9 and Voyager  with my parents....as I suspect many other people did.  90s trek appealed to a wide age range.   To make modern trek have M rated shows and then a kid's show and another for teens/young adults is stupid.

author: @vincentcollins998
text: I have always been a large Star Trek fan. More so I have liked the shows and movies that remained close to Gene Rodenberry's initial portrayal of what the future may have been like.  The JJ Abram's remakes, I did not find too awful. It was Star Trek content and I got lost in the story after all, admiring the different perspective, but also at the same time finding distinct things in the story that I did not necessarily like. I think Kurtzman may have had his name on some of the television serials that I watched, but I have not seen anything recently because I do not own a subscription to Paramount Plus. I do agree if they do want to reboot the franchise and take it in the same direction that Rodenberry may have envisioned, I would not be upset; I think it would be something interesting to watch :)

author: @bluestripetiger
text: The problem is that Kurtzman has either never understood what makes Trek tick or he's simply uninterested in learning we need an entire writer's room of serious sci-fi writers. What we've gotten from Kurtzman has been space opera and comedy. He is uninterested in exploring ideas.
